concepts.title: Finding Greatest coefficient
concepts.conceptBody:
Multinomial Theorem

For any positive integer $n$,

\(\left(x_4+x_2+\ldots x_r\right)^n=\sum_{\alpha_1+\alpha_2 \ldots \alpha_r=n} \frac{n !}{\alpha ! \alpha_{2} ! \alpha_{3} ! \ldots \alpha_{r} !} x_1^{\alpha_1} x_2^{\alpha_2} \ldots x_r^{\alpha_r}\)

For example,

in the same expansion of ${{(x+y+z)}^{10}}$ coefficient of ${{x}^{3}}{{y}^{2}}{{z}^{5}}$ will be $\frac{10!}{2!3!5!}$ since while multiplying $x+y+z$ ten times one will get ${{x}^{3}}{{y}^{2}}{{z}^{5}}$, as many number of times as is the number of ways of arranging xxxyyzzzzz in a line.

Note : Binomial Theorem follows from multinomial theorem by putting $\text{n}=2$.

Example 

Find the coefficient of ${{x}^{3}}{{y}^{4}}{{z}^{2}}$ in the expansion of ${{(2x-3y+4z)}^{9}}$. 

Solution: 

General Term in ${{(2x-3y+4z)}^{9}}$

$~=9!/\left( {{\text{n}}_{1}}!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{n}}_{2}}!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{n}}_{3}}! \right)\cdot {{(2\text{x})}^{\text{n}1}}\cdot {{(-3\text{y})}^{\text{n}2}}\cdot {{(4\text{z})}^{\text{n}3}}$

$~=9!/\left( {{\text{n}}_{1}}!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{n}}_{2}}!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{n}}_{3}}! \right)\cdot {{2}^{\text{n}1}}{{(-3)}^{\text{n}2}}{{(-3)}^{\text{n}2}}\cdot {{(4)}^{\text{n}3}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{\text{n}1}}\cdot {{\text{y}}^{\text{n}2}}\cdot {{\text{z}}^{\text{n}3}}$

Putting $\text{n}1=3,\text{n}2=4,\text{n}3=2$

= $9!/(3!$ $4!$ $2!)\cdot {{2}^{3}}{{(-3)}^{4}}\cdot {{(4)}^{2}}\cdot {{\text{x}}^{3}}{{\text{y}}^{4}}{{\text{z}}^{2}}$

= $\left[ 9.8.7.6.5.4!/\left( 3.2.1\cdot 4!\cdot 2 \right) \right]/(3.2.1.$ $4!.2)]$ 8 81 $16{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{\text{y}}^{4}}{{\text{z}}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow $ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Coefficient }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{3}}{{\text{y}}^{4}}{{\text{z}}^{2}}=9$ 8 7 5 8 81 8

= 13063600

Greatest coefficient in the expansion of ${{\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\ldots \ldots \ldots +{{\text{x}}_{\text{m}}} \right)}^{\text{n}}}$

$=n!/{{(q!)}^{m-r}}{{[\left( q+1 \right)!]}^{r}}$

where ' $q$ ' is the quotient and ' $r$ ' is the remainder, when ' $n$ ' is divided by ' $m$ '.

Ex : Find the greatest coefficient in the expansion of ${{(a+b+c+d)}^{15}}$ 

Sol. : Here $\text{n}=15,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}=4$

$15/4$ is quotient 3 and remainder 3.

since $q=3$ and $r=3$

Hence greatest - coefficient $=15!/{{\left[ {{(3!)}^{4-3}}\times \left( 3+1 \right)! \right)}^{3}}$

$\left. =15!/\left[ 3! \right)\times {{(4!)}^{3}} \right]$

$~=15\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ! }\!\!~\!\!\text{ / }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (3! }\!\!~\!\!\text{ 4! }\!\!~\!\!\text{ 4! }\!\!~\!\!\text{ 4!) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Example

Find the coefficient of ${{x}^{7}}$ in the expansion of ${{\left( 1+3x-2{{x}^{3}} \right)}^{10}}$. 

Solution: 

General Term in ${{\left( 1+3\text{x}-2{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)}^{10}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}
{} & ~=10!/\left( {{\text{n}}_{1}}!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{n}}_{2}}!{{\text{n}}_{3}}! \right){{(1)}^{\text{n}}}{{~}_{1}}{{(3\text{x})}^{\text{n}}}{{~}_{2}}{{\left( -2{{\text{x}}^{3}} \right)}^{\text{n}}}{{~}_{3}} \\
\end{array}$

= $10 ! /\left(\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{n}_1 & \mathrm{n}_{2} ! & \left.\mathrm{n}_{3} !\right) \cdot 3 \mathrm{n}_3{ }^{\mathrm{n} 2}(-2)^{\mathrm{n} 3} \mathrm{x}_2{ }^{\mathrm{n}+3 \mathrm{n}}{ }_3\end{array}\right.$

Where ${{\text{n}}_{1}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}=10$……. (i)

For coefficient of ${{x}^{7}}:{{n}_{2}}+3{{n}_{3}}=7$….. (ii)

From (ii), possible non-negative integral values of ' ${{n}_{2}}$ ' and ' ${{n}_{3}}$ ' are :

${{\text{n}}_{2}}$ $=7,{{\text{n}}_{3}}=0$ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ since }\!\!~\!\!\text{ from }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (i) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ : }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ ${{\text{n}}_{1}}=3$

${{\text{n}}_{2}}=1,{{\text{n}}_{3}}=2$ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ since }\!\!~\!\!\text{ from }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (i) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ : }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ ${{\text{n}}_{1}}=7$

Or \({{\text{n}}_{2}}=4,{{\text{n}}_{3}}=1\)\(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ since }\!\!~\!\!\text{ from }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (i) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ : }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\)

\({{\text{n}}_{1}}=5\)

So required coefficient of ${{x}^{7}}$ : (will get it by adding the above 3 cases)

$10\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ! }\!\!~\!\!\text{ / }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( 3!\times 7!\times 0! \right)\cdot {{(3)}^{7}}{{(-2)}^{0}}+10!/\left( 7!\times 1!\times 2! \right)\cdot {{(3)}^{1}}{{(-2)}^{2}}+10!/\left( 5!\times 4!\times 1! \right)\cdot {{3}^{4}}{{(-2)}^{1}}$

(10. 9.87 !) / (7!.3.2.1)37 + (10.9.8. 7!) / (7!. 2) x 3 x $4-[(10.9.8.7.6.5$ !) / (5!. 4.3.2.1.)].3.2

$~=10\times 9\times {{43}^{6}}+10\times 9\times 4\times 3\times 4-10\times 9\times 7\times 6\times {{3}^{3}}\times 2$

$~=10\times 9\times 4\left( 36+12-7\times 34 \right)$

$~=360\times \left( 729+12-567 \right)$

$~=62640$

Example

Find the total number of terms in the expansion of ${{(x+y+z+w)}^{n}},n\in N$ 

Solution: 

The number of terms in the expansion of ${{(x+y+z+w)}^{n}}$ is ${{~}^{n+4-1}}{{C}_{4-1}}$.

$~={{~}^{\text{n}+3}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$

$~=\frac{\left( \text{n}+3 \right)\left( \text{n}+2 \right)\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}{6}$

You can learn this

Alternative method 

We know that

\(\begin{aligned}
& (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{w})^{\mathrm{n}}=\{(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})+(\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{w})\}^{\mathrm{n}} \\
& = (\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^{\mathrm{n}}+{ }^n C_1\left((\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^{\mathrm{n}-1}(\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{w})+{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_2(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y})^{\mathrm{n}-2}(\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{w})^2+\ldots \ldots \cdot{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{z}+\mathrm{w})^{\mathrm{n}}\right.
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore\) Number of terms in RHS
\(\begin{aligned}
& =(\mathrm{n}+1)+\mathrm{n} \cdot 2+(\mathrm{n}-1) \cdot 3+\ldots \ldots+1(\mathrm{n}+1) \\
& =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}+1)(\mathrm{r}+1) \\
& =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{n}+1)+\mathrm{nr}-\mathrm{r}^2) \\
& =(\mathrm{n}+1) \sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}} 1+\mathrm{n} \sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}-\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{r}^2 \\
& =(\mathrm{n}+1) \cdot(\mathrm{n})+\mathrm{n} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6} \\
& =\frac{(\mathrm{n}+1)(\mathrm{n}+2)(\mathrm{n}+3)}{6}
\end{aligned}\)
